title: Pruning Compact ConvNets for Efficient Inference

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - PRUNING COMPACT CONVNETS FOR EFFICIENT INFER ENCE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Neural network pruning is frequently used to compress over-parameterized networks by large amounts, while incurring only marginal drops in generalization performance. However, the impact of pruning on networks that have been highly optimized for efficient inference has not received the same level of attention. In this paper, we analyze the effect of pruning for computer vision, and study state-ofthe-art ConvNets, such as the FBNetV3 family of models. We show that model pruning approaches can be used to further optimize networks trained through NAS (Neural Architecture Search). The resulting family of pruned models can consistently obtain _better_ performance than existing FBNetV3 models at the same level of computation, and thus provide state-of-the-art results when trading off between computational complexity and generalization performance on the ImageNet benchmark. In addition to better generalization performance, we also demonstrate that when limited computation resources are available, pruning FBNetV3 models incur only a fraction of GPU-hours involved in running a full-scale NAS.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Neural networks frequently suffer from the problem of _over-parameterization_ , such that the model can be compressed by a large factor to drastically reduce memory footprint, computation as well as energy consumption while maintaining similar performance. This is especially pronounced for models for computer vision (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014), speech recognition (Pratap et al., 2020) and large text understanding models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2018). The improvements obtained from intelligently reducing the number of model parameters has several benefits, such as reduction in datacenter power consumption, faster inference and reduced memory footprint on edge devices such as mobile phones which also enable decentralized techniques ex. federated learning (Kairouz et al., 2019).

There are several techniques to reduce model size while maintaining similar generalization performance, such as model quantization (Polino et al., 2018), NAS (Neural Architecture Search) (Elsken et al., 2019) and model distillation through teacher-student networks (Gou et al., 2021). For the scope of this paper, we consider pruning as a technique to remove trainable weights in the network, and save on computation costs for the FBNet family of models. The motivations for this are two-fold. Firstly, state-of-the-art models such as FBNet (Wu et al., 2019) already adopt the best practices in the area of efficient hardware-aware design of convolutional neural network based models, and are widely used across different vision tasks. This makes them suitable baselines to understand whether pruning can offer any performance gain over their already optimized behavior. While there has been limited work on pruning for efficient convolution network models they investigate older architectures such as EfficientNet and MobileNet (Aflalo et al., 2020) or integrate pruning into expensive techniques such as joint prune-and-architecture search (Wang et al., 2020).

For each of the constituent models of the FBNetV3 family (FBNetV3A, FBNetV3B,..., FBNetV3G) we reduce the number of parameters using two pruning based approaches: (1) _Global magnitudebased pruning_ : Starting with the pre-trained model, we prune all weights whose magnitude is below a threshold chosen in order to achieve a target number of FLOPs for the pruned model; (2) _Uniform magnitude-based pruning_ : Starting with the pre-trained model, we prune weights in each layer whose magnitude is below a level-specific threshold in order to yield a pruned model achieving a target number of FLOPs with the same sparsity in each layer. After either pruning method is applied, we

fine-tune the pruned model for a certain number of epochs until convergence is reached. Within the scope of our study in this paper, we are mostly interested in the following research questions:

- **RQ1** : Pruning to improve computation vs. performance tradeoff. Can a model obtained by pruning a larger FBNetV3 model **M1** (optimized using NAS) achieve higher generalization performance than a smaller FBNetV3 model **M2** when the pruned model has the same number of FLOPs as **M2** ?

- **RQ2** : Pruning as an efficient paradigm. When a larger FBNetV3 model **M1** is available and computational resources are limited, is pruning a faster and less computationally expensive approach to obtain a model with higher accuracy at a desired computation level (FLOPs) than running a full-fledged architecture search?

_Pruning to improve computation vs. performance tradeoff (_ _**RQ1** )._ There have been recent research advances in the area of building hardware-aware efficient models (Deng et al., 2020). These can provide good generalization performance while adhering to constraints on memory, inference latency and battery power, which are often dictated by the hardware environment where inference happens. Experiments described in existing work on efficient vision models such as ChamNet (Dai et al., 2019), MobileNet (Howard et al., 2017), EfficientNet (Tan & Le, 2019) and FBNetV2 (Wan et al., 2020) have shown that it is possible to achieve even higher performances on standard image recognition tasks such as ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) at a certain level of FLOPs. However the efficient design of these models does not solve the over-parameterization problem completely, and none of these approaches study how model pruning can be performed to obtain even better trade-offs between computation and model accuracy. This paper is the first of its kind to understand how we can improve on the state-of-the-art in this problem space.

_Pruning as an efficient paradigm (_ _**RQ2** )._ In addition to achieving state-of-the-art performance with reduced FLOPs, we are also interested in understanding how such pruned models can be obtained _inexpensively_ with limited resources that are generally available to a machine learning practitioner who has access to existing optimized models but limited computing resources. For example, the FBNetV3 models are freely available through Facebook’s Mobile Model Zoo[1] , while EfficientNet models can be obtained at GitHub[2] . While the techniques needed to obtain computation- and latencyfriendly models have been democratized through open-sourcing the source code as well as the models themselves, fully applying these techniques necessitates costly operations such as finding an optimal network topology through meta-learning approaches (You et al., 2020) and search algorithms such as Genetic Algorithms (GAs) (Goldberg & Deb, 1991).

Given the high-degree of intractability of this problem, expensive computational resources are often needed in this case, easily exceeding the budget available to a university research laboratory or an angel-stage startup (Zoph & Le, 2016). When a starting model is already available, for example through open-sourcing, the best option would be to perform a cheap modification of the model to fit a certain target FLOPs/latency requirement. In this paper we have compared the NAS approaches for training FBNetV3 models with our pruning techniques on a computational complexity metric (GPU-hours) to effectively answer **RQ2** .

_Benchmark results._ In addition to experimental outcomes for answering **RQ1** and **RQ2** , we also benchmark pruned FBNetV3 models using available open-sourced quantized sparse kernels and conduct ablation studies to obtain additional insights into pruning performance. These results augment our main observations and demonstrate that with existing hardware support, it is possible to deploy pruned cutting-edge computer vision models with practical latency reductions and improve further beyond the performance vs. FLOPs trade-off.

We conduct our experiments on ImageNet, which is an object-recognition task on a large training dataset of 1.2 million images. We show that computationally less intensive techniques such as uniform and global magnitude-based pruning of larger FBNetV3 models can yield higher test accuracies than small models while having the same number of FLOPs. Given a target computation budget for an efficient model, we show that it is more practically advantageous (both in terms of performance and running time) to simply prune the larger model than run a neural architecture search to find the target model from scratch.

The technique we have employed for pruning (unstructured sparsity) is already tried and tested, however our novelty lies in studying whether efficient image recognition models such as FBNetV3 can be optimized further to improve on the FLOPs-accuracy curve, and the contributions are two fold : (1) FBNets are themselves state-of-the-art in efficient vision models and we achieve better accuracy-FLOPs tradeoff over these models and (2) from the standpoint of computational overhead, we significantly reduce the amount of GPU hours required to obtain such models. Pruning a publicly available NAS optimized model incurs _≈_ 4x less GPU hours to achieve a target FLOPs level, compared to training a full-fledged NAS to obtain a model which has less accuracy at the same FLOPs level.

_Paper organization._ The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we describe related work in the area of efficient vision model design and also provide an introduction to different pruning techniques. In Section 3, we discuss our experimental setup, including a description of the baseline models and the _global_ and _uniform_ pruning approaches we have employed. Section 4 describes our main findings and we conclude the paper in Section 5.

## 2 RELATED WORK

We discuss related literature in the areas of _computationally efficient vision models_ and _model pruning_ . Within the scope of our work, we mainly focus on inference efficiency of models in contrast to training efficiency.

_Computationally efficient vision models:_ Neural networks for computer vision are generally characterized by convolutional layers and fully-connected layers, along with blocks such as residual or skip connections. This makes such networks resource intensive in terms of FLOPs, which affects the memory storage and power consumed, and also leads to increased latency. It is of paramount importance to design more efficient networks which can provide higher performance for the same FLOPs or latency level, or even to optimize them appropriately to provide the same performance at reduced FLOPs/latency. This can be performed either through the design of new simplified layers, for example in deep residual learning (He et al., 2016) or though explicit model compression as in weight quantization (Polino et al., 2018). Extremely deep networks for image recognition often suffer from not only high complexity and inference latency, but also from the issue of _vanishing gradients_ (Pascanu et al., 2013). This was addressed through deep residual networks which effectively simplified network design through skip-connections. MobileNets (Howard et al., 2017) are one of the earlier approaches to building small low-latency networks by using depthwise separable convolutions with two parameters, _width_ and _resolution_ multipliers. They demonstrate the effectiveness of MobileNets across different vision tasks, such as face embeddings and object detection. MobileNetV2 (Sandler et al., 2018) extends MobileNets by utilizing inverted residual filter structures and linear bottlenecks, obtaining improvements on state-of-the-art models both in terms of accuracy and computational complexity. ShuffleNets (Zhang et al., 2018) propose dedicated residual units where 1 _×_ 1 convolutions are replaced with pointwise group convolutions and channel shuffling reducing FLOPs computations.

More recently, the focus on building efficient neural network models has shifted to techniques that treat the design of efficient networks as a search problem, falling under the umbrella of Neural Architecture Search (NAS). EfficientNets (Tan & Le, 2019) propose a novel scaling method which adjusts the network’s length, width, and resolution to optimize performance subject to target memory and FLOPs constraints. They also define a novel baseline that is optimized by a multi-objective neural architecture search. The FBNet collections of models—FBNet (Wu et al., 2019), FBNetV2 (Wan et al., 2020) and FBNetV3 (Dai et al., 2021)—employ neural architecture search to obtain highly-optimized models that improve on the state-of-the-art for different visual understanding tasks. FBNet frames the architecture search as a differentiable meta-learning problem with gradient based techniques, namely _DNAS_ —Differentiable Neural Architecture Search—by Wu et al. (2019), and avoids selecting the optimized model over a discrete set. The subsequent entry in this collection, FBNetV2, expands the search space over conventional DNAS, and employs a masking scheme to maintain the same level of computational complexity while searching over this expanded space. FBNetV3 further improves on the state-of-the-art by employing Neural Architecture Recipe Search (NARS) and searching over the space of not only architectures, but also corresponding recipes (which are generally hyper-parameters). In this paper, we consider FBNetV3 models as our baselines as they are state-of-the-art. We are interested in understanding if they are overparameterized and evaluate how much model pruning can improve performance at a certain FLOPs level over the state-of-the-art in this family of models.

_Model Pruning:_ Modern neural networks, particularly those processing complex sensory inputs (such as speech, vision and language) for perception applications, are often over-parameterized. It is only to be expected that we should be able to compress such networks significantly to maintain the same level of performance at decreased level of computation (fewer weights and reduced FLOPs), memory footprint and power consumption. Foundational efforts in this space include the _Optimal Brain Surgeon_ (Hassibi & Stork, 1993) and _Optimal Brain Damage_ (LeCun et al., 1990). Recently the idea of network pruning has been formalized through the lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carbin, 2018), which claims that randomly initialized, feed-forward networks have winning sub-networks that perform just as well as the original network on an unseen test dataset. Model pruning is generally of two types: unstructured and structured pruning. Unstructured pruning, as the name suggests, doesn’t adhere to any structure and prunes neurons based on chosen criteria (such as magnitude). This has the advantage of providing higher performance, but is difficult to implement in hardware, as it needs dedicated support for efficient sparse matrix multiplications. Meanwhile, structured pruning is the practice of removing entire groups of neurons (e.g., blocks within the weight matrix, or channels in convolutional neural networks). This is easy to implement without dedicated hardware support, but has the issue of lower generalization performance than unstructured pruning (Yao et al., 2019). In the literature, there have also been several studies, for example investigating whether rewinding (training from scratch with a fixed mask) can perform just as well as the fine-tuning on top of the original unpruned network (Renda et al., 2020). Blalock et al. (2020) provide an overview survey of recent advances and open problems in neural network pruning.

In the research area of designing efficient networks for computer vision, there has not been much focus on understanding how pruning can be applied to the current generation of models. Most literature on pruning is based on older networks such as VGGNet, ResNet (He et al., 2016), and MobileNet (Sandler et al., 2018). Our work improves upon these existing studies by understanding how pruning can improve the FLOPs-accuracy tradeoff over existing state-of-the-art networks.

## 3 PRUNING TECHNIQUES AND SETUP

In this section, we describe the main components of our techniques and experimental setup, including _Baseline Models_ , _Pruning Techniques_ , _Latency Measurement_ and _Metrics_ . We have mainly used standard splits of the ImageNet dataset, further details are in Section A.1 of the appendix.

## 3.1 BASELINE MODELS

Dai et al. (2020) address the previous limitations of NAS-based architecture search where these approaches can only search over architectures given a training recipe (set of hyperparameters), and thus cannot optimize over both. As described in Section 2, the most recent state-of-the-art models are based on NARS (Neural Architecture-Recipe Search), which we select as baseline models. Table 3 lists the accuracy of FBNetV3 models (Dai et al., 2021) on the ImageNet classification task, along with the number of model parameters and computation complexity in terms of FLOPs.

Each baseline model consists of multiple IRF (Inverted Residual Filter) blocks, which contain convolutional layers of different kernel sizes. For our experiments, we are mostly interested in 1 _×_ 1 convolutions as potentially prunable, since within each FBNetV3 model, the 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers constitute >80% of total model FLOPs for all models in the family, and the open-sourced sparsity kernel support we use for latency benchmarking is available only for fully connected layers. A 1 _×_ 1 convolution can be transformed into an equivalent fully connected layer with a few tensor reshape operations without any significant loss of performance or latency.

For each initial and target FBNetV3 model _X_ and _Y_ , where _X_ is larger than _Y_ , we prune _X_ to a _sparsity level_ of _S_ so that the FLOP count is the same as for _Y_ . The number of FLOPs consumed by a linear layer of sparsity _S_ is proportional to the number of sparse matrix multiplications performed and is given by _S ∗ F_ , where _F_ is the corresponding dense FLOPs. Thus if _F_ 1 _×_ 1( _X_ ) is the number of FLOPs consumed by the 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers and _F_ ( _x_ ) is the total number of FLOPs consumed by model _X_ , we have:

required to prune each larger FBNetV3 model to a smaller one based on Eq. (1). For global pruning, (1) does not hold, and we compute the target sparsities empirically from the layer shapes instead with details provided in Section A.2. We prune each larger FBNetV3 model to a discrete FLOPs target based on a defined set of smaller models in the family, and not to a continuous range of FLOPs values, as it makes it easier to compare models directly based on a given computation budget. If we can demonstrate that for the same computation level, the pruned larger FBNetV3 model has higher performance than a smaller model with the same FLOPs, it is sufficient to demonstrate that we can improve on the FLOPs-accuracy curve over the state-of-the-art.

## 3.2 PRUNING TECHNIQUES

In this paper, we utilize a pre-trained FBNetV3 model with higher number of FLOPs without training an image classification model from scratch with sparsity, which would be time consuming and computationally intensive. There are several approaches in the literature such as prune-and-finetune (Han et al., 2015) and iterative pruning with sparsity scheduling (Frankle & Carbin, 2018). We have utilized the former for our experiments, as although studies have shown that iterative and incremental pruning approaches lead to better generalization performance, they typically require training for high number of epochs, need tuning and selection of optimal sparsity schedules and are computationally resource intensive. We have therefore not considered them in our experiments. For our prune and fine-tune experiments, we have used 8-GPU boxes, with each box having Nvidia V100 (Volta) 32G GPUs. As described in Section 1, we perform both global and magnitude-based pruning experiments. For the latency benchmarking, we also perform magnitude-based uniform pruning with a sparse block size of 1 _×_ 4 as explained in Section 3.3.

We have conducted a hyper-parameter tuning for the learning rate parameter, with LR values in the set {4e-5, 8e-5, 1.6e-4}, as fine-tuning generally admits smaller learning rates than training from scratch. We have found that using the same learning rate for all models, along with the same hyper-parameter settings used for training the seed model is sufficient to obtain pruned networks which are superior to the baseline FBNetV3 models. Hence minimal hyper-parameter tuning was required for our experiments and we have used values of settings such as weight decay and momentum to be the same as those used for training the baseline FBNetV3 models. During fine-tuning after pruning, we have used a smoothed validation loss to stop the process early after a convergence tolerance (0.01%) is reached between two consecutive epochs. Generally, we have observed fine-tuning to converge around _∼_ 250 epochs.

## 3.3 LATENCY MEASUREMENTS AND METRICS

We are interested not only in the sparsity level of our pruned models and the image recognition performance, but also in metrics which potentially improve due to model sparsity, such as number of parameters, the FLOP count and the model latency. For reporting model performance under pruning, we use standard image recognition metrics such as Top-1 and Top-5 test accuracies. We measure overall model sparsity, which is different to the layer sparsity since we only prune 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers, as explained in Section 3.1. We report the model FLOPs, because this metric captures the computational footprint of the model and its power consumption.

Last, we record the total latency (in ms.) under pruning. The sparse kernels used in our experiments are already in open-source and released under the PyTorch sparse quantization library[3] . Prior to using these kernels, we perform uniform layer-wise block-based pruning with block sizes of 1 _×_ 4. Magnitude based pruning is implemented at block level, and the model is quantized to 8-bit integers (int8) before latency benchmarking, which is performed on Intel CPUs designed using the Skylake micro-architecture. While we would expect sparsity to translate to tangible inference speedups, this is highly dependent on the sparse kernel support provided by hardware. Current hardware is not wellsuited for unstructured randomly sparse matrix multiplications and tend to do better with structured sparsity in models (Anwar et al., 2017). We have utilized block sparsity within the weight matrix for latency experiments. However this often tends to come at a decreased level of model performance. The design of highly performant sparse models under structured sparsity with reasonable inference speedups remains an important research topic outside the scope of this paper.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 PRUNED FBNETV3 MODEL PERFORMANCE

To answer **RQ1** , we consider the family of FBNetV3 models as baselines and seed models for further pruning. For each pair of models _X_ , _Y_ in the family, we calculate the amount of sparsity required to prune the larger model _X_ to a model that consumes the same number of FLOPs as the target smaller model _Y_ , via Equation 1. There are 21 potential seed and target model pairs, however we conduct pruning experiments only for a depth of 2 for tractability. For example, given FBNetV3E as the seed, we only prune it to FLOPs targets corresponding to FBNetV3D and FBNetV3C. Table 1 presents the accuracy and number of parameters of the pruned models at each target FLOPs level. The improvement in performance is apparent even at lower FLOPs targets, where we might expect baseline models such as FBNetV3A to not be over-parameterized. For example, pruning FBNetV3C to a target of 356.6 MFLOPs obtains a network which is 1.43% better than FBNetV3A. Figure 1 plots the Top-1 ImageNet testing accuracy vs. FLOPs for the best pruned models as seen from Table 1. This clearly shows that pruning FBNetV3 models with minimal fine-tuning can significantly improve on the state-of-the-art for FLOPs vs. Top-1 accuracy trade-off. This analysis is performed for both uniform layer-wise and global magnitude-based prune with fine-tune settings. Global pruning ranks the weights of the entire network in contrast to uniform layer-wise pruning, which ranks each layer’s weights to determine the sparsity mask. It would be expected that global pruning performs better than uniform pruning for the same target sparsity level or number of non-sparse parameters. However in our experiments we determine the pruning threshold based on FLOPs targets, and find global pruning to require higher sparsity levels, which results in uniform pruning outperforming global pruning in Top-1 ImageNet accuracy in most cases.

## 4.2 PRUNING COMPLEXITY

In addition to demonstrating the improvement over state-of-the-art obtained by pruning FBNetV3 models, it is also important to quantify the reduction in computational complexity obtained in pruning a larger FBNetV3 model compared to training an FBNetV3 model directly through NAS (Network Architecture Search). **RQ2** (pruning for efficient model search) asks if the pruning and subsequent fine-tuning approach in Section 4.1 is faster than a full-fledged neural architecture search. During pruning and subsequent fine-tuning, we train the pruned networks till the validation loss converges to within a pre-specified tolerance, as described in Section 3.2. The time needed is generally less than when training the original FBNetV3 models, which runs for 400 epochs. The results are quite conclusive—we not only obtain pruned models superior in performance to the original neural search optimized models, but also as described in Section 1, computational cost is significantly lower when starting from a pre-trained model with higher FLOPs. Given the performance improvements obtained with lower computational resources, this approach is beneficial for an experimental setting where researchers have access to open-sourced pre-trained models and limited GPU resources, for example in a small startup or an academic environment. We observe that the degree of speedup reduces as the network size gets bigger (e.g., in FBNetV3A vs. FBNetV3C) due to higher training time to convergence. Nevertheless, we still obtain a speedup of 3-5 times compared to a full NAS (Neural Architecture Search).

## 4.3 LATENCY EXPERIMENTS

We also measure the latency-performance tradeoff for the pruned FBNetV3G models. FBNetV3G is the largest model in the family and so is expected to have the best generalization performance under high sparsity levels. As described in Section 3.3, we prune the network using block sparsity (where the block size is 1 _×_ 4) to sparsity levels in the set {40%, 50%, 60%}. We have not utilized lower sparsity levels, as we have observed that for the selected kernels we need at least 40% sparsity to yield any significant latency benefits. We have pruned all 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers uniformly here and subsequently converted them to fully-connected layers for compatibility with the quantized sparse kernels. In Figure 2a, we present the Top-1 ImageNet accuracy vs. latency curve

The pruned FBNetV3G models show marked performance reduction with lower latency as expected, with a sparsity level of 60% translating to around 7% absolute accuracy reduction with a latency reduction of 18 ms (16% relative). While the 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers account for >80% of FLOPs, they only constitute 25% of overall network latency. This is consistent with previous literature (Dudziak et al., 2020) which shows that computational complexity (ex. FLOPs) and latency are not well-correlated, and indeed the latter is more dependent on layer shapes. This result underscores the need to develop more latency-friendly pruning techniques which can potentially improve on the state-of-the-art in this domain.

## 4.4 INSIGHTS INTO PRUNING EXPERIMENTS

Our pruning experiments demonstrate that we can improve on the state-of-the-art FBNetV3 models in generalization performance for a given FLOPs level. In this subsection, we obtain an insight into (1) the sparsity pattern under global magnitude-based pruning and (2) the sensitivity of each layer when pruned in isolation under uniform layer-wise magnitude pruning (sparsity level of 95%). For (1) in Figure 2b, we plot the amount of sparsity obtained per 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer. The model being considered is an FBNetV3E network pruned to a sparsity level of 43.6%, to the same FLOPs level as FBNetV3C and subsequently fine-tuned. We note that the sparsity level in lower layers is lower which is potentially required for maintaining the performance . Higher sparsity can be admitted in upper layers of the network where it has learnt more redundant representations. SE (Squeeze and Excitation) 1 _×_ 1 convolution layers generally tend to get pruned more compared to other layers, with the sparsity being >99% for two such SE layers in stage _xif_ 5_0. This indicates that we can also consider revisiting SE layer role in FBNetV3 networks, and even remove entire layers in future work to yield additional latency and FLOPs benefits.

For analysis (2) we prune each 1 _×_ 1 convolution layer in isolation at a sparsity target of 95% and record the Top-1 test accuracy obtained on ImageNet dataset. For each type of layer, PW:expansion, PWL: bottleneck, SE: Squeeze-Excitation we plot the distribution of accuracies in Figure 2d. We observe that the PW and PWL layers are most sensitive to high sparsity, while SE layers are able to retain performance adequately. We could also avoid pruning the most sensitive layers (appearing as outliers in the figure) to maintain generalization performance. This observation corroborates findings from analysis (1), and motivates us to revisit the role of squeeze-excitation layers in future work.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we have investigated the problem of improving on the current state-of-the-art FLOPs vs. performance trade-off for FBNets which have been pre-optimized by NAS (Neural Architecture Search). We have employed network pruning techniques, and our results demonstrate that we can further improve on performance over FBNetV3 at a given FLOPs target through global as well as uniform magnitude-based pruning. This happens not only for relatively over-parameterized networks such as FBNetV3G, but also smaller networks such as FBNetV3A which have lower computational complexity. On average, the GPU-hours incurred during pruning is about _∼_ 4 _×_ less than that consumed by a full-scale NAS. We have also performed latency measurements on the FBNetV3G model and conducted an analysis to understand the sparsity patterns and sensitivity of different FBNetV3 layers to pruning. For future work, we plan to investigate squeeze-excitation layers in more detail, and explore structured pruning approaches such as channel and layer pruning to further improve on the latency-performance tradeoff for this family of models.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper tackles a timely and relevant problem—whether pruning can further improve already optimized compact ConvNets like FBNetV3. The core idea of leveraging pruning as a low-cost alternative to NAS is compelling, especially for resource-limited settings. The results suggest consistent accuracy gains over baseline models at matched FLOPs, and the 3–5× computational savings over NAS are promising. However, several methodological and framing issues undermine the strength of the conclusions.

Overview issues:

- Inadequate ablation isolates pruning from fine-tuning effects: The paper attributes performance gains entirely to pruning, but provides no ablation to disentangle the contribution of fine-tuning alone. Since the pruned models are fine-tuned from a larger model's weights, the gains could stem from better initialization or extended training rather than sparsity. For example, retraining the target model (e.g., FBNetV3A) from scratch with the same fine-tuning regimen might close the accuracy gap. Without such a control, the claimed benefits of pruning are confounded. This is especially critical given that the authors use the same hyperparameters as the original training, which may not be optimal for a smaller model. A proper ablation would compare: (1) pruning + fine-tuning, (2) no pruning + fine-tuning (i.e., training target model from larger weights), and (3) standard training of target model.

- Latency measurements use synthetic block sparsity and unrealistic hardware: The latency results in Section 4.3 are based on block $1\times 4$ sparsity applied only to $1\times 1$ convolutions, converted to fully connected layers. This setup does not reflect real-world deployment, where structured sparsity must be preserved across full convolutional layers and hardware-aware kernels are required. Moreover, the latency is measured on Intel Skylake CPUs using quantized sparse kernels, but FBNetV3 models are designed for mobile GPUs and edge ASICs. The paper notes that $1\times 1$ convolutions contribute only 25% of total latency despite 80% of FLOPs, which further weakens the practical impact of pruning them. Readers will question whether the reported 16% latency reduction translates to real mobile devices. The evaluation should include latency on mobile GPUs (e.g., Adreno, Mali) or accelerators (e.g., Edge TPU) with native sparsity support.

- FLOPs reduction is conflated with actual efficiency gains: The paper relies heavily on FLOPs as a proxy for computational cost, but Figure 2a and the discussion confirm that FLOPs and latency are poorly correlated. This undermines the central premise: improving the FLOPs-accuracy curve may not yield real speedups. The authors acknowledge this in passing but do not adjust their evaluation framework. A more meaningful analysis would optimize for latency directly, either by incorporating latency into the pruning target or reporting Pareto curves on real hardware. The current approach risks promoting models that are efficient in FLOPs but slow in practice—a known pitfall in efficient model design. The contribution would be stronger if the pruning criterion were latency-aware rather than FLOPs-based.

- Pruning criteria are not adapted to compact architectures: The paper applies magnitude-based pruning—effective for over-parameterized models—to FBNetV3, which is already highly optimized. However, recent work suggests that such criteria may not be optimal for compact models, where weights are already near-minimal. The authors do not justify why magnitude remains appropriate here. For instance, group-Lasso or geometry-aware criteria might better preserve performance under aggressive sparsity. The lack of comparison to alternative criteria (e.g., Taylor expansion, activation-based) weakens the methodological contribution. Given that FBNetV3 uses neural architecture recipe search, the sensitivity of pruning to architectural priors (e.g., SE layers) should also be explored more systematically, rather than just observed post hoc.

- Data leakage risk in iterative pruning and fine-tuning: The prune-and-fine-tune pipeline uses the validation set to determine convergence (via smoothed validation loss), but the same set is later used to report test accuracy. This creates a risk of data leakage and overfitting to the validation split, especially since fine-tuning stops based on validation performance. The paper does not clarify whether a separate validation set was used for early stopping or if the test set was used indirectly. For ImageNet, standard practice is to use a held-out validation set for tuning and report test accuracy only once. Without a three-way split (train/val/test) or cross-validation, the reported accuracies may be optimistic. The authors should either clarify the split usage or re-run experiments with a dedicated validation set for early stopping.

- Overstated generality of findings across model scales: The paper claims broad applicability of pruning across the FBNetV3 family, but Table 1 shows that pruning FBNetV3G to match FBNetV3E actually *harms* performance (−0.16% and −1.68% for uniform and global pruning). This exception is downplayed in the discussion, but it contradicts the central narrative. The degradation suggests that the largest model may not be over-parameterized or that pruning disrupts learned representations critical for generalization. The paper should acknowledge this limitation and analyze why pruning fails at the high end. A more nuanced claim—e.g., pruning helps for mid-sized models but not the largest—would be more credible and informative.

- Lack of latency benchmarks on target hardware: The latency measurements are conducted on Intel Skylake CPUs using quantized sparse kernels applied to reshaped 1×1 convolutions, but FBNetV3 models are designed for mobile and edge accelerators such as mobile GPUs (Adreno, Mali) or NPUs. Moreover, only 25% of network latency comes from 1×1 convolutions, so pruning them—even aggressively—has limited real-world impact. The paper should include latency measurements on representative mobile hardware with native sparsity support, such as Qualcomm Hexagon or Samsung NPU, and evaluate end-to-end inference speed. This would demonstrate whether the FLOPs reduction translates into actual deployment benefits, which is essential for a paper claiming practical efficiency gains.

- FLOPs-accuracy trade-off lacks hardware-aware validation: The central evaluation metric—FLOPs—is poorly correlated with actual inference latency, as the authors themselves note. Yet the entire pruning strategy is based on matching FLOPs to smaller FBNetV3 models, and the main claim rests on improved accuracy at equal FLOPs. This risks optimizing a proxy that does not reflect real efficiency. The paper should either report latency-accuracy curves on real devices or incorporate hardware-measured latency directly into the pruning target. At minimum, it should compare the pruned models against the baselines in terms of actual speed (ms) on a common edge platform. Without this, the contribution remains theoretical and may not generalize to practical deployment scenarios.

- No analysis of pruning failure at high capacity: Table 1 shows that pruning FBNetV3G to match FBNetV3E results in worse accuracy under both pruning methods (−0.16% and −1.68%), contradicting the paper’s general claim. This suggests that the largest model may not be over-parameterized or that pruning disrupts critical features. The authors briefly mention future work on SE layers but do not analyze why pruning fails here. They should investigate whether performance loss stems from over-pruning key components (e.g., SE layers, early convolutions) or from insufficient fine-tuning budget. A targeted ablation—e.g., pruning only non-SE layers or using layer-adaptive thresholds—would clarify the limits of the approach and strengthen the paper’s credibility by acknowledging and explaining boundary conditions.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a controlled ablation comparing pruning + fine-tuning versus fine-tuning alone (without pruning) to isolate the contribution of sparsity to accuracy gains.

- Report latency measurements on mobile or edge hardware (e.g., mobile GPU, Edge TPU) using realistic sparsity patterns and convolutional kernels, not just CPU with converted FC layers.

- Replace FLOPs-accuracy curves with latency-accuracy trade-offs on real devices to better reflect practical efficiency.

- Include a comparison of pruning criteria beyond magnitude (e.g., Taylor, activation-based) to justify the choice for compact models.

- Clarify validation set usage and ensure no data leakage; ideally, use a separate validation split for early stopping.

comments.title: Claim of consistent improvement contradicted by Table 1
comments.quote: The resulting family of pruned models can consistently obtain *better* performance than existing FBNetV3 models at the same level of computation, and thus provide state-of-the-art results when trading off between computational complexity and generalization performance on the ImageNet benchmark.
comments.explanation: The word 'consistently' implies uniform gains across all model scales, but Table 1 shows that pruning FBNetV3G to match FBNetV3E degrades accuracy by −0.16% (uniform) and −1.68% (global). This exception invalidates the claim of universal improvement. The abstract should reflect that benefits are model-scale-dependent—e.g., 'Pruned models often achieve better performance'—to avoid overstating generality.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for sparsity calculation
comments.quote:
Thus if $F_{1\times 1}(X)$ is the number of FLOPs consumed by the 1$\times$1 convolution layers and $F(x)$ is the total number of FLOPs consumed by model $X$, we have:

$S=(F(X)-F(Y))/F_{1\times 1}(X)$ (1)
comments.explanation: Equation (1) is algebraically incorrect. The correct expression for sparsity $S$ that ensures the pruned model matches $F(Y)$ is $S = (F(Y) - F(X) + F_{1\times 1}(X)) / F_{1\times 1}(X)$. The current formula can yield $S > 1$, which is invalid, and misrepresents how FLOPs reduction is achieved. This undermines the core method for setting pruning targets and must be corrected.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of state-of-the-art FLOPs-accuracy trade-off
comments.quote: Figure 1 plots the Top-1 ImageNet testing accuracy vs. FLOPs for the best pruned models as seen from Table 1. This clearly shows that pruning FBNetV3 models with minimal fine-tuning can significantly improve on the state-of-the-art for FLOPs vs. Top-1 accuracy trade-off.
comments.explanation: The claim of improving the state-of-the-art lacks external validation. Figure 1 includes only FBNetV3 variants and their pruned versions, omitting established compact models like MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-Lite, or TinyNet. Without comparison to these baselines, there is no evidence that the pruned models exceed prior Pareto-optimal trade-offs. The assertion should be revised or supported with a broader comparison.

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of 7% accuracy drop
comments.quote: The pruned FBNetV3G models show marked performance reduction with lower latency as expected, with a sparsity level of  $60\%$  translating to around  $7\%$  absolute accuracy reduction with a latency reduction of  $18~\mathrm{ms}$  ( $16\%$  relative).
comments.explanation: The phrase '7% absolute accuracy reduction' is misleading. If FBNetV3G has ~80.4% baseline accuracy, a 7% absolute drop would imply ~74.8%, far below the ~78% shown in Figure 2a. The authors likely mean a 7-percentage-point drop. This misuse inflates the perceived performance loss. Rewrite as '7-percentage-point accuracy drop' for accuracy.

comments.title: Inconsistent sparsity levels in text vs. figure
comments.quote: We prune the network using block sparsity (where the block size is  $1 \times 4$ ) to sparsity levels in the set  $\{40\%, 50\%, 60\% \}$ .
comments.explanation: The text states sparsity levels are {40%, 50%, 60%}, but Figure 2a includes a data point beyond 60%, suggesting evaluation at 70%. This discrepancy between reported setup and visualized results undermines experimental transparency. Either update the text to include 70% or remove the outlier point to ensure alignment.

comments.title: False corroboration between pruning analyses
comments.quote: This observation corroborates findings from analysis (1), and motivates us to revisit the role of squeeze-excitation layers in future work.
comments.explanation: The claim that analysis (2) corroborates analysis (1) is logically flawed. Analysis (1) shows SE layers are naturally pruned to high sparsity under global pruning; analysis (2) shows they tolerate forced 95% isolated sparsity. The first reflects weight magnitude, the second robustness to perturbation—they are distinct phenomena. One does not validate the other. Remove the claim of corroboration and present them as independent observations.

comments.title: SE layer sparsity misinterpreted as redundancy
comments.quote: SE (Squeeze and Excitation)  $1 \times 1$  convolution layers generally tend to get pruned more compared to other layers, with the sparsity being  $>99\%$  for two such SE layers in stage  $xif5\_0$ . This indicates that we can also consider revisiting SE layer role in FBNetV3 networks, and even remove entire layers in future work to yield additional latency and FLOPs benefits.
comments.explanation: High sparsity in SE layers under magnitude pruning does not imply redundancy. SE weights are often small due to normalization and rescaling, not lack of importance. Prior work confirms their positive impact on generalization. The suggestion to remove them based solely on pruning behavior is unsupported. Temper the conclusion and acknowledge that functional importance requires ablation, not just sparsity patterns.

comments.title: Inconsistent sparsity definition across pruning methods
comments.quote:
Hence, sparsity measures the fraction of 1$\times$1 convolution weights removed, and so higher sparsity indicates a smaller model. For the uniform pruning

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

required to prune each larger FBNetV3 model to a smaller one based on Eq. (1). For global pruning, (1) does not hold, and we compute the target sparsities empirically from the layer shapes instead with details provided in Section A.2.
comments.explanation: The paper defines sparsity over 1×1 weights but implies different computation methods for uniform and global pruning. This raises ambiguity: is sparsity still defined only on 1×1 layers in global pruning? If not, comparisons become invalid. Clarify that both methods prune only 1×1 layers and define sparsity consistently, as only these layers are transformed to FC and supported by sparse kernels.

comments.title: FLOPs used as proxy despite poor latency correlation
comments.quote: We report the model FLOPs, because this metric captures the computational footprint of the model and its power consumption.
comments.explanation: FLOPs do not reliably capture computational footprint or power consumption, especially under sparsity. As the paper itself notes, FLOPs and latency are poorly correlated due to memory, kernel, and hardware effects. Relying on FLOPs risks optimizing a misleading proxy. Revise the claim to acknowledge that FLOPs are a rough estimate and that real efficiency depends on hardware-specific factors.

comments.title: NAS cost incorrectly assumed constant across targets
comments.quote: In Table 2, for each of the best performing uniformly-pruned models in Section 4.1 we report the number of GPU-hours consumed by the prune and fine-tune strategy, along with the GPU-hours consumed when obtaining a FBNetV3 model through architecture search using the method described in Dai et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: The paper assumes NAS requires 10.7k GPU-hours for all targets (A, C, E), but this contradicts standard practice where search cost varies by model complexity and evaluation budget. If 10.7k is the total cost amortized over many models, attributing it fully to each target inflates speedup claims. Clarify whether this is per-model cost or shared, and justify accordingly.

comments.title: Total cost of pruning ignores pre-training expense
comments.quote: The results are quite conclusive—we not only obtain pruned models superior in performance to the original neural search optimized models, but also as described in Section 1, computational cost is significantly lower when starting from a pre-trained model with higher FLOPs.
comments.explanation: The computational comparison omits the cost of training the original large model (e.g., FBNetV3G) used as the pruning seed. If that model was obtained via NAS (10.7k GPU-hours), the total cost exceeds direct NAS. The paper compares only incremental costs, not end-to-end. This misrepresents efficiency. Acknowledge that total cost may be higher, though reuse offers practical advantages in constrained settings.

comments.title: Validation-based early stopping risks data leakage
comments.quote: During fine-tuning after pruning, we have used a smoothed validation loss to stop the process early after a convergence tolerance (0.01%) is reached between two consecutive epochs.
comments.explanation: Using smoothed validation loss for early stopping, without clarifying whether a separate validation set was used for accuracy reporting, risks overfitting. The same set should not inform both training duration and final evaluation. For ImageNet, standard practice requires strict train/val/test separation. Clarify the split usage or confirm that a held-out set was used for convergence monitoring.

comments.title: Layer sensitivity test uses inconsistent sparsity regime
comments.quote: For analysis (2) we prune each  $1 \times 1$  convolution layer in isolation at a sparsity target of  $95\%$  and record the Top-1 test accuracy obtained on ImageNet dataset.
comments.explanation: The 95% sparsity target in the sensitivity test is far more aggressive than the 43.6% global sparsity used in main experiments. Pruning layers in isolation at such high rates does not reflect actual pruning dynamics, where compensation occurs across layers. This makes the analysis misleading. Use a sparsity level aligned with observed global patterns or acknowledge the regime mismatch.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of FBNetV3's NARS methodology
comments.quote: FBNetV3 further improves on the state-of-the-art by employing Neural Architecture Recipe Search (NARS) and searching over the space of not only architectures, but also corresponding recipes (which are generally hyper-parameters).
comments.explanation: The paper misrepresents NARS by equating 'recipes' with generic hyper-parameters like learning rate. In Dai et al. (2021), recipes are structured architectural patterns (e.g., layer configurations, connectivity rules), not standard training hyper-parameters. This mischaracterization underestimates the sophistication of the baseline. Revise to accurately reflect that recipes are architectural design templates, not conventional hyper-parameters.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a study on pruning state-of-the-art compact ConvNets (FBNetV3 family) to improve accuracy-FLOPs trade-offs and reduce computational costs compared to full NAS. While the core results show promise, the methodology suffers from several critical issues that undermine the validity and practical relevance of the claims.

The paper addresses an important question about whether NAS-optimized models can be further compressed through pruning. The authors demonstrate that pruned models can outperform smaller FBNetV3 baselines at equivalent FLOPs levels and achieve significant computational savings over NAS. However, the experimental design has serious flaws in latency measurement methodology, inadequate baseline comparisons, and questionable generalization claims that limit the paper's impact.

Overview issues:

- Latency measurement methodology lacks hardware diversity: The latency experiments in Section 4.3 are conducted only on Intel Skylake CPUs using a specific sparse kernel library. This single-platform evaluation is insufficient for a paper claiming practical efficiency improvements. The paper acknowledges that FLOPs don't correlate well with latency but fails to demonstrate how the pruned models perform on mobile GPUs/TPUs where FBNetV3 models are typically deployed. Without measurements on target mobile hardware (e.g., Snapdragon processors, Apple Neural Engine), the claimed latency improvements lack practical relevance. The 16% relative speedup at 60% sparsity may not generalize to hardware architectures with different sparse matrix multiplication capabilities.

- Missing comparison with state-of-the-art compact architectures: The paper only compares pruned FBNetV3 models against other FBNetV3 variants, failing to benchmark against the broader landscape of efficient architectures. For a paper claiming state-of-the-art results, it's essential to include comparisons with MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-B0 to B3, and other recent compact models at similar FLOPs levels. The authors mention these models in related work but never present comparative results. This omission makes it impossible to assess whether the pruning approach truly advances the state-of-the-art or merely exploits weaknesses within the FBNetV3 family.

- Unstructured pruning results don't translate to practical deployment: The main accuracy improvements in Table 1 are achieved through unstructured pruning, which the paper itself acknowledges is difficult to implement efficiently in hardware (Section 3.3). The latency experiments using block sparsity (1×4) show much smaller gains and significant accuracy drops (7% absolute at 60% sparsity). This disconnect between the impressive FLOPs-accuracy results and the modest latency improvements undermines the paper's practical contributions. The authors should clearly separate these two aspects and not imply that the unstructured pruning results are deployable without specialized hardware support.

- Insufficient statistical validation of results: The paper reports single-run accuracy numbers without any indication of variance or statistical significance. Pruning experiments involve stochastic processes (weight initialization, fine-tuning), yet the results lack error bars or confidence intervals. For claims like 'pruning FBNetV3C to 356.6 MFLOPs obtains a network which is 1.43% better than FBNetV3A', readers need to know whether this improvement is consistent across multiple runs or within experimental variance. The paper should include standard deviations across multiple seeds and statistical tests to validate the significance of reported gains.

- Limited pruning depth selection lacks justification: The authors restrict experiments to a 'depth of 2' for tractability (Section 4.1), meaning they only prune models to FLOPs levels of models two steps smaller in the family. This arbitrary constraint prevents understanding of how far pruning can be pushed and whether the improvements hold for more aggressive compression ratios. For example, pruning FBNetV3G directly to FBNetV3A levels could reveal whether the approach scales. This limitation should be explicitly acknowledged as a constraint on the claims rather than buried in methodology.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous results: The paper claims pruning improves on FBNetV3's FLOPs-accuracy trade-off but never shows this improvement is meaningful. A concrete demonstration would involve comparing against a baseline where pruning fails - for instance, showing that when applied to a model already at its computational optimum (like a randomly initialized FBNetV3 pruned to the same FLOPs), the accuracy drops significantly. This would establish that the observed gains aren't just artifacts of fine-tuning but genuine improvements from pruning over-parameterized NAS-optimized models. Without such a counterexample, readers cannot assess whether the 1-2% accuracy gains are substantive or expected from any fine-tuning.

- No worked example of sparsity computation: Equation (1) defines sparsity calculation but the paper never walks through a complete numerical example. For instance, it should compute the exact sparsity level when pruning FBNetV3D (644.4 MFLOPs) to match FBNetV3B (461.6 MFLOPs), showing the intermediate values of F(X), F(Y), and F_{1×1}(X). This would help readers verify the implementation and understand why uniform pruning requires different sparsities than global pruning. Without this concrete calculation, the methodology remains abstract and difficult to reproduce.

- Underdeveloped latency-accuracy trade-off implications: The paper discovers that 1×1 convolutions account for >80% of FLOPs but only 25% of latency, yet doesn't explore what this means for practical pruning strategies. It should compute the theoretical maximum latency reduction achievable by pruning only the latency-critical layers versus FLOPs-critical layers. For example, if pruning the remaining 75% of latency-contributing layers at 40% sparsity yields only 5% FLOPs reduction but 15% latency savings, this would demonstrate the importance of latency-aware pruning. The current analysis stops at observation without developing actionable insights.

- Missing structured pruning baseline comparison: While the paper mentions structured pruning as more hardware-friendly, it never provides a baseline comparison. It should include at least one structured pruning experiment (e.g., channel pruning) on the same FBNetV3 models to quantify the accuracy-latency trade-off difference. For instance, showing that uniform channel pruning at 30% sparsity achieves 0.5% accuracy loss with 10% latency reduction would contextualize the 7% accuracy loss at 60% sparsity for block pruning. Without this comparison, readers cannot evaluate whether unstructured pruning's accuracy benefits justify its hardware costs.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add latency measurements on mobile hardware platforms (e.g., Snapdragon, Apple Neural Engine) to demonstrate practical deployment benefits

- Include comparisons with MobileNetV3 and EfficientNet baselines at equivalent FLOPs levels to establish true state-of-the-art claims

- Report statistical significance with multiple runs and error bars for all pruning experiments

- Clearly separate unstructured pruning accuracy results from structured pruning latency results to avoid misleading implications

- Extend pruning experiments beyond depth-2 constraint or explicitly justify this limitation as part of the scope

comments.title: Missing results section content
comments.quote:
4 RESULTS

# 4.1 PRUNED FBNETV3 MODEL PERFORMANCE
comments.explanation: The section header '4 RESULTS' is present but the actual results content appears to be missing from the provided text. This is a critical omission since this section should contain the experimental findings, performance metrics, and comparisons that validate the paper's claims about pruning FBNetV3 models.

comments.title: Sparsity-FLOPs relationship error
comments.quote: The number of FLOPs consumed by a linear layer of sparsity $S$ is proportional to the number of sparse matrix multiplications performed and is given by $S*F$, where $F$ is the corresponding dense FLOPs.
comments.explanation: This relationship is incorrect. For a sparse matrix with sparsity $S$, the computational cost is approximately $(1-S)*F$ FLOPs, not $S*F$. Sparsity $S$ represents the fraction of weights removed, so the remaining computation is $(1-S)$ of the original. This error affects the understanding of how pruning reduces computation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated 4x GPU-hours savings claim
comments.quote: Pruning a publicly available NAS optimized model incurs $\approx$4x less GPU hours to achieve a target FLOPs level, compared to training a full-fledged NAS to obtain a model which has less accuracy at the same FLOPs level.
comments.explanation: The paper makes a specific quantitative claim about 4x reduction in GPU hours but provides no supporting evidence or methodology. This claim needs to be backed up with actual GPU hour measurements from both approaches, including hardware specifications, training times, and number of runs.

comments.title: Missing latency measurements in results
comments.quote: Last, we record the total latency (in ms.) under pruning. The sparse kernels used in our experiments are already in open-source and released under the PyTorch sparse quantization library.
comments.explanation: The section claims to record total latency measurements but Table 1 only contains accuracy and sparsity data. There are no latency values reported despite the detailed description of the latency measurement methodology. Either add latency measurements to Table 1 or provide a separate table with the latency results.

comments.title: Missing baseline latency for context
comments.quote: ed, with a sparsity level of  $60\%$  translating to around  $7\%$  absolute accuracy reduction with a latency reduction of  $18~\mathrm{ms}$  ( $16\%$  relative). Wh
comments.explanation: The section reports latency reductions but never provides the baseline latency of the unpruned FBNetV3G model. Without this baseline, readers cannot assess whether the reported improvements are meaningful or verify the consistency of the reported percentages. Add the baseline latency value to enable proper interpretation.

comments.title: Overly broad claim about pruning NAS-optimized networks
comments.quote: The resulting family of pruned models can consistently obtain *better* performance than existing FBNetV3 models at the same level of computation, and thus provide state-of-the-art results when trading off between computational complexity and generalization performance on the ImageNet benchmark.
comments.explanation: The claim that pruned models 'consistently obtain better performance' is too strong given the limited experimental scope. The paper only experiments with FBNetV3 models and restricts pruning depth to 2 levels. This narrow evaluation cannot support a general claim about 'consistently' better performance across NAS-optimized networks.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about being first to study pruning
comments.quote: This paper is the first of its kind to understand how we can improve on the state-of-the-art in this problem space.
comments.explanation: This claim is factually incorrect. The literature on pruning efficient models is well-established, with numerous papers studying pruning of MobileNet, EfficientNet, and other compact architectures. The authors should either remove this claim or rephrase it to specify their specific contribution (e.g., first to study pruning of FBNetV3 models).

comments.title: Inconsistent pruning method description
comments.quote: (2) *Uniform magnitude-based pruning*: Starting with the pre-trained model, we prune weights in each layer whose magnitude is below a level-specific threshold in order to yield a pruned model achieving a target number of FLOPs with the same sparsity in each layer.
comments.explanation: The description contains a logical contradiction: it claims to use 'level-specific thresholds' but then states the goal is 'the same sparsity in each layer.' If each layer has the same target sparsity, then the thresholds would be identical across layers, not 'level-specific.' This creates confusion about whether uniform pruning means uniform sparsity targets or uniform threshold values.

comments.title: Unjustified experimental limitation
comments.quote: There are 21 potential seed and target model pairs, however we conduct pruning experiments only for a depth of 2 for tractability.
comments.explanation: The paper limits experiments to 'a depth of 2' for tractability but doesn't explain what 'depth' means in this context or why this specific limitation was chosen. Without this justification, readers cannot assess whether the limited experiments adequately represent the full potential of the pruning approach.

comments.title: Inconsistent gain calculation in Table 1
comments.explanation: The gain calculation for the G→E row appears inconsistent with the formula used elsewhere. For uniform pruning, 81.166% - 81.3% = -0.134%, not -0.16%. For global pruning, 79.934% - 81.3% = -1.366%, not -1.68%. These discrepancies suggest either calculation errors or inconsistent rounding that should be corrected.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates whether pruning techniques, traditionally applied to over-parameterized models, can improve the performance-FLOPs trade-off of already-optimized NAS-based ConvNets like FBNetV3. The authors demonstrate that pruning these compact models can yield modest gains in accuracy and reduce the computational cost compared to running a full NAS pipeline.

The paper addresses a practical and relevant question regarding the limits of model optimization. While the empirical results show some improvements, the work relies heavily on FLOPs as a proxy for efficiency, which is often misleading for modern hardware. The study would be stronger with a more rigorous treatment of real-world latency and a clearer distinction between theoretical complexity and actual hardware performance.

Overview issues:

- Reliance on FLOPs as a Proxy for Latency: The paper consistently uses FLOPs as the primary metric for efficiency and as the basis for pruning targets in Section 3.1. However, as the authors acknowledge in Section 4.3, FLOPs do not correlate well with wall-clock latency on modern hardware. Since the core claim is 'Efficient Inference,' using FLOPs as the sole optimization target is insufficient. The paper should report Pareto-frontiers based on measured latency on target hardware (e.g., mobile CPUs) rather than just FLOPs, as the latter ignores memory access patterns and kernel overheads that dominate inference time in compact models.

- Lack of Comparison with Standard Baselines: The comparison is limited to the FBNetV3 family itself. While this shows that pruning can improve upon a specific NAS-generated baseline, it does not compare the resulting models against other standard, highly optimized efficient networks of similar latency (e.g., MobileNetV3 or EfficientNet-Lite) that might have been trained with better recipes. To demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, the authors must show that their pruned FBNetV3 models outperform other architectures at the same real-world latency, not just at the same FLOP count.

- Inconsistent Treatment of Unstructured vs. Structured Pruning: The paper mixes unstructured pruning (Section 3.2) with block-based structured pruning for latency experiments (Section 3.3). The performance gains reported in Table 1 are based on unstructured pruning, which generally does not provide speedups on standard hardware without specialized kernels. The paper should clarify that the accuracy gains from unstructured pruning are largely theoretical in terms of inference speedup, and explicitly separate the accuracy results for structured vs. unstructured methods to avoid conflating potential accuracy with actual achievable efficiency.

- Limited Analysis of Fine-tuning Costs: The authors claim that pruning is more efficient than NAS (RQ2) because it requires fewer GPU-hours. However, the comparison in Table 2 only considers the training time of the pruned model and ignores the cost of the initial 'seed' model training or the search time that produced the seed model. If the cost of obtaining the large seed model is included, the total computational budget for the pruning pipeline might be much closer to that of a full NAS. A more transparent accounting of the total 'cradle-to-grave' compute cost is needed to support the claim that pruning is a significantly cheaper paradigm.

- Lack of Pareto-frontier analysis on real hardware: The paper relies heavily on FLOPs to define pruning targets and evaluate efficiency, yet admits in Section 4.3 that FLOPs correlate poorly with actual wall-clock latency. To substantiate the claim of 'Efficient Inference,' the authors must provide a Pareto-frontier plot showing Top-1 Accuracy vs. measured latency on a standard mobile or edge device (e.g., ARM-based CPU or mobile GPU). This is necessary because the overhead of sparse kernels, memory bandwidth limits, and cache locality—which dictate real-world performance—are entirely ignored by the FLOPs metric. Without this, the reader cannot determine if the 'efficient' models are actually faster in practice.

- Incomplete accounting of total computational cost: The claim that pruning is a more efficient paradigm than NAS (RQ2) rests on the comparison in Table 2, which only considers the fine-tuning phase. This omits the significant computational cost of training the large 'seed' model from scratch, which is a prerequisite for the pruning pipeline. To provide a fair assessment of the 'cradle-to-grave' compute budget, the authors should include the training cost of the seed model in their comparison against the full NAS pipeline. Failure to do so misrepresents the total resource requirement for a practitioner starting from a random initialization.

- Missing comparison against non-NAS optimized baselines: The paper evaluates pruning only against other members of the FBNetV3 family. While this shows that pruning can optimize an existing architecture, it does not demonstrate that the resulting models are competitive with other standard, highly-optimized efficient architectures (e.g., MobileNetV3 or EfficientNet-Lite) at the same latency. To support the claim of state-of-the-art performance, the authors should include at least one such standard baseline in their accuracy-latency plots. This would clarify whether the observed gains are specific to the FBNetV3 search space or if they generalize to other efficient ConvNet designs.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Replace or augment the FLOPs-based Pareto analysis with measured latency on target hardware (e.g., mobile CPU/GPU) to validate the efficiency claims.

- Include a comparison against other state-of-the-art efficient architectures (e.g., MobileNetV3) at equivalent latency levels, rather than just comparing within the FBNetV3 family.

- Provide a more comprehensive accounting of total compute costs, including the pre-training of the seed models, to justify the 'efficiency' claim of the pruning paradigm.

- Clearly distinguish between accuracy results achieved via unstructured pruning and those achieved via structured pruning, as the latter is the only one that yields immediate inference speedups on standard hardware.

comments.title: Incomplete accounting of total computational cost
comments.quote: In Table 2, for each of the best performing uniformly-pruned models in Section 4.1 we report the number of GPU-hours consumed by the prune and fine-tune strategy, along with the GPU-hours consumed when obtaining a FBNetV3 model through architecture search using the method described in Dai et al. (2020).
comments.explanation: The comparison of computational costs in Table 2 is incomplete because it only accounts for the fine-tuning phase of the pruning pipeline, while the NAS cost includes the full training of the architecture. To accurately compare the 'cradle-to-grave' compute budget, the cost of training the initial 'seed' model must be added to the pruning and fine-tuning cost. Without including the seed model training time, the reported speedup of 3-5x is misleading. Please add a row or footnote to Table 2 that includes the pre-training cost of the seed models to provide a fair comparison against the full NAS pipeline.

comments.title: Inconsistent logic regarding global versus uniform pruning performance
comments.quote: It would be expected that global pruning performs better than uniform pruning for the same target sparsity level or number of non-sparse parameters. However in our experiments we determine the pruning threshold based on FLOPs targets, and find global pruning to require higher sparsity levels, which results in uniform pruning outperforming global pruning in Top-1 ImageNet accuracy in most cases.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that global pruning performs worse because it requires 'higher sparsity' to meet a FLOPs target is logically inconsistent. Global magnitude pruning is a superset of uniform pruning; if the global method were truly optimal, it could choose to mimic the uniform mask, thereby achieving at least the same accuracy. The observed performance degradation suggests that the global pruning implementation is either failing to converge to the optimal mask or is being penalized by the specific fine-tuning schedule used. Please rewrite the explanation to acknowledge that the global pruning implementation likely suffers from optimization instability or sub-optimal mask selection rather than attributing the performance gap to the inherent nature of global pruning.

comments.title: Inconsistent FLOPs reduction formula
comments.quote:
Thus if $F_{1\times 1}(X)$ is the number of FLOPs consumed by the 1$\times$1 convolution layers and $F(x)$ is the total number of FLOPs consumed by model $X$, we have:

$S=(F(X)-F(Y))/F_{1\times 1}(X)$ (1)
comments.explanation: The definition of $S$ as a sparsity level is mathematically inconsistent with the provided formula. If $F$ is the dense FLOP count, the remaining FLOPs after pruning a fraction $S$ of the 1x1 layers should be $F(X) - S \cdot F_{1\times 1}(X)$. Setting this equal to the target FLOPs $F(Y)$ yields $F(Y) = F(X) - S \cdot F_{1\times 1}(X)$, which rearranges to $S = (F(X) - F(Y)) / F_{1\times 1}(X)$. However, the text defines $S$ as 'sparsity level' and then states the FLOPs consumed by a layer of sparsity $S$ is $S*F$. If $S$ is the fraction removed, the remaining FLOPs should be $(1-S)*F$. Please clarify if $S$ represents the fraction of weights removed or the fraction of weights retained, and ensure the formula for FLOPs consumed by a sparse layer matches the definition of $S$.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of state-of-the-art performance
comments.quote: The resulting family of pruned models can consistently obtain *better* performance than existing FBNetV3 models at the same level of computation, and thus provide state-of-the-art results when trading off between computational complexity and generalization performance on the ImageNet benchmark.
comments.explanation: The claim that these models provide 'state-of-the-art results' is not supported, as the comparison is restricted solely to the FBNetV3 family. To justify a claim of state-of-the-art performance on the ImageNet benchmark, the paper must demonstrate that these models outperform other established efficient architectures, such as MobileNetV3 or EfficientNet-Lite, at equivalent computational budgets. Comparing only against variants of the same architecture family merely demonstrates the effectiveness of the pruning method within that specific search space. Please qualify this claim by specifying that the results are state-of-the-art relative to the FBNetV3 baseline family.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis
comments.quote: Recently the idea of network pruning has been formalized through the lottery ticket hypothesis *(Frankle and Carbin, 2018)*, which claims that randomly initialized, feed-forward networks have winning sub-networks that perform just as well as the original network on an unseen test dataset.
comments.explanation: The description of the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (LTH) is slightly inaccurate. The LTH specifically posits that these 'winning tickets' are sub-networks that, when trained in isolation from their initial weights, reach test accuracy comparable to the original network. The current phrasing implies that the sub-networks perform well immediately upon being found, without explicitly mentioning the necessity of training them from their original initialization. Clarifying this distinction is important because the LTH is fundamentally about the trainability of sparse sub-networks, not just their existence or performance at initialization.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper investigates pruning FBNetV3 models to improve FLOPs-accuracy tradeoffs and reduce computational costs compared to NAS. The work addresses a relevant gap in understanding how pruning interacts with NAS-optimized compact architectures. The empirical setup is straightforward, and the observation that larger pruned models can outperform smaller baselines at matched FLOPs is a reasonable finding. However, the deployment narrative is weakened by hardware measurement limitations, narrow baselines, and methodological inconsistencies that require substantial revision before the claims can be fully trusted.

The paper tackles a practical problem in model compression by examining pruning on NAS-optimized compact ConvNets. The empirical results show consistent accuracy gains over FBNetV3 baselines at matched FLOPs, and the fine-tuning pipeline is computationally lighter than full NAS. The work demonstrates solid engineering intuition and provides useful ablation studies on layer sensitivity. However, the gap between theoretical FLOPs reduction and actual hardware latency, combined with limited baseline comparisons and reporting gaps, prevents the paper from meeting top-venue standards in its current form.

Overview issues:

- FLOPs-centric efficiency claims vs. hardware reality: The paper heavily promotes FLOPs reduction as the primary proxy for inference efficiency, yet Section 4.3 explicitly demonstrates that latency does not scale linearly with FLOPs on the tested Intel Skylake CPUs. The authors note that 1x1 convolutions account for over 80% of FLOPs but only 25% of actual latency, which directly contradicts the framing of pruning these layers as a path to faster deployment. Readers will question the practical value of the proposed method if the target hardware lacks optimized sparse kernels. The contribution reads as a theoretical FLOPs optimization rather than a deployment-ready compression technique. To resolve this, the authors should either benchmark latency on widely available edge accelerators or reframe the paper strictly as a FLOPs-reduction study, clearly separating computational complexity from wall-clock speed.

- Narrow baseline comparisons: Table 1 restricts comparisons exclusively to the FBNetV3 family, which leaves the state-of-the-art claim under-supported. The pruning literature has advanced significantly, with structured channel pruning, magnitude-based filter removal, and joint optimization methods routinely outperforming simple magnitude pruning on compact architectures. Without comparing against contemporary pruning baselines or other efficient models like MobileNetV3 or EfficientNet-Lite, the paper cannot establish whether the observed accuracy gains stem from the pruning strategy or merely from the larger parameter budget of the seed models. The authors need to benchmark against at least three recent pruning methods and two external efficient architectures to validate the core premise that pruning FBNetV3 yields superior FLOPs-accuracy tradeoffs.

- Misleading GPU-hour comparison for RQ2: Table 2 compares the GPU-hours of pruning fine-tuning against the training time of a single NAS-optimized model, which misrepresents the computational cost of neural architecture search. Full NAS pipelines require searching over thousands of candidate architectures, evaluating them, and often training multiple checkpoints before selecting a final model. Reporting only the final model training time inflates the perceived speedup of the pruning approach. The claimed 4.77x reduction conflates search overhead with fine-tuning efficiency. The authors should report the total search-and-train hours for the NAS baseline, or reframe RQ2 to focus strictly on the fine-tuning budget available to practitioners with limited compute.
